text.title: Launch Readiness — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for the 8-gate readiness review, owner discipline, and the go/no-go threshold.

rule.statement: The review evaluates EXACTLY 8 gates: SECURITY, PERFORMANCE, OBSERVABILITY, RUNBOOKS, ON-CALL-ROTATION, LEGAL, SUPPORT-ENABLEMENT, COMMS-TREE. Skipping a gate (e.g. "we don't need on-call") forces explicit risk acceptance + sign-off; gates cannot be silently omitted.

rationale.source: Google SRE PRR + AWS Well-Architected + observed launch-failure post-mortems

rule.rationale: These 8 gates cover 90% of post-launch surprises across 5 years of public post-mortems (Heroku, Cloudflare, Linear, Notion). Reducing gates without explicit risk acceptance reproduces the historical failure modes.

rule.statement: Each gate MUST have ONE named owner (single human, not "team"). Owner is responsible for the pass/fail signal AND for fixing any blocker. Multiple-owner gates devolve into "everyone thought someone else was doing it".

rationale.source: DACI / RACI accountability research + post-mortem root-cause analysis

rule.rationale: Diffuse ownership is the leading cause of unaddressed launch risks. Naming a single human eliminates the "I thought you were on it" failure.

rule.statement: Each gate has HARD pass criteria — measurable, binary outcomes. Examples: SECURITY → "SAST + dependency audit clean OR documented exceptions"; OBSERVABILITY → "p50/p95/p99 dashboard live AND alerting configured at SLO violation"; RUNBOOKS → "≥ 1 runbook per top-3 incident scenario URL returns 200". Vague criteria ("we have docs") are rejected.

rationale.source: Google SRE PRR rubric + outage post-mortems

rule.rationale: Soft criteria let teams self-rubber-stamp. Hard binary criteria force confrontation with reality (e.g. "is the runbook URL actually live?").

rule.statement: Owners sign off WITHIN the review tool with timestamp and explicit `signed_by`. Verbal "yes we're good" or Slack thumbs-up are NOT sign-offs. Sign-off < 48h before launch is fresh; older sign-offs MUST be re-confirmed.
rule.rationale: Verbal sign-offs evaporate under post-mortem scrutiny. Time-stamped sign-off in the tool of record is the only durable artifact.

rule.statement: Any single gate with `status: blocker` vetoes the launch regardless of other gates' green status. The PM may NOT "compensate" for a blocker by stronger sign-off on other gates. Blockers route to a remediation plan with new launch-date estimate.

rationale.source: Toyota andon cord production line + Etsy's

rule.rationale: Any-one-can-stop-the-line is the only discipline that respects real risk. Compensation logic ("we're solid everywhere else") is post-hoc rationalisation.

example.title: OBSERVABILITY gate pass criteria
example.code: Gate: OBSERVABILITY Owner: jane.doe@acme.com (SRE lead) Criteria (ALL must hold): ✅ p50/p95/p99 latency dashboard live URL: ... ✅ Error-rate alert configured at 1% violation 5min sustained ✅ SLO defined: 99.9% successful requests, 200ms p95 ✅ Cardinality budget defined for new metrics ✅ Trace sampling enabled at 1% prod / 100% staging Status: PASS (signed_by: jane.doe, 2026-05-19 14:30 UTC)
example.note: r3 + r4: hard criteria, timestamped sign-off.

example.title: Vague gate (r3 violation)
example.code:
Gate: RUNBOOKS Owner: "DevOps team" (r2 violation — not a named human) Criteria: "We have docs in Notion" Status: PASS (signed: "looks good!" in #launch slack)
Post-launch incident: page-on-call has no runbook URL. Runbook in Notion is 6 months old, references retired infra.
example.note: r2 + r3 + r4 all violated. Vague pass = guaranteed surprise.
